planting.note: Start seeds indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost or plant transplants after all frost danger has passed; in zone 9 plant transplants March–May. Torenia fournieri is one of the few annual flowers that genuinely tolerates and performs well in partial to full shade — making it an invaluable substitute for impatiens in shaded situations. Clown Rose is a compact selection in the Clown series bred for uniform growth, compact habit (8–12 inches), and excellent heat tolerance — an improvement over older, leggier torenia selections that declined in zone 9 summer heat. In zone 9 torenia performs most reliably in spring and fall in full shade; summer heat may reduce flowering but the plant recovers when temperatures moderate. Self-seeds modestly in zone 9, providing some volunteer reseeding.

directions: Plant in partial to full shade in well-drained, fertile, humus-rich, consistently moist soil — Torenia is one of the few annual flowers that genuinely performs in full shade, making it invaluable for deep-shade situations where most flowering annuals cannot maintain blooming; Clown Rose Torenia is a compact selection of Wishbone Flower (Torenia fournieri), a shade-tolerant tropical annual from Vietnam producing an unusual, bicolor, tubular-to-open flower in rose-pink with a deeper rose-pink lower lip and a bright yellow center spot — a color combination that is vivid and cheerful in the low-light conditions of deep shade where these flowers are most practical. The common name 'Wishbone Flower' comes from the two curved stamens that are fused at the tips inside the flower, forming a wishbone shape visible when the flower is examined closely. Torenia's genuine shade tolerance — it requires shade in hot climates and will bleach and decline in direct sun — makes it one of a very small group of annual flowers that can deliver consistent, colorful bloom in the deep shade under trees, on north-facing walls, and in covered garden structures where no direct sun reaches. In zone 9, where summer shade garden color is particularly difficult to maintain, Torenia provides one of the most reliable warm-season flowering options for genuinely shaded situations from spring through early fall.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Torenia fournieri for Florida Shade Gardens
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Annual Shade Flowers for Gulf South
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Heat-Tolerant Shade Annuals for Zone 9

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 10–12 weeks before transplanting; do not cover seeds, as they require light for germination. Transplant outdoors in partial shade as soon as nighttime temperatures remain above freezing; in zone 9 plant September–November for fall and winter bloom, or February–March for spring bloom before summer heat arrives. Crystal Palace is one of the oldest and most widely grown lobelia selections — a compact, mounding (not trailing) form with deep violet-blue flowers and characteristically deep bronze-green foliage that distinguishes it from other blue lobelia selections. In zone 9 lobelia blooms most profusely in the cooler months (October–April); it declines and may die out in July–August heat. Use as a cool-season shade annual rather than attempting summer performance.

directions: Plant in partial shade — morning sun with afternoon shade is ideal in zone 9; deep shade reduces flowering — in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral, consistently moist soil with good drainage; Crystal Palace is one of the most widely grown and historically important compact lobelia cultivars, producing a dense, mounding form covered with the richest, most intensely saturated deep violet-blue flowers of any annual bedding plant commonly available — a color so intense and pure that Crystal Palace lobelia has become the standard reference for deep blue in annual bedding plant design. The characteristic deep bronze-green foliage (rather than the light green of other lobelia cultivars) provides an additional visual distinction that enhances the contrast with the deep blue flowers. Lobelia erinus produces some of the most saturated true-blue color available from any annual flower — a blue so deep and vivid that it provides an invaluable cool-color contrast anchor in mixed planting compositions with yellows, oranges, reds, and whites. In zone 9 Crystal Palace lobelia is primarily a fall, winter, and spring annual — providing exceptional cool-season color October through April in shaded and partial-shade situations where other cool-season annuals (pansies, snapdragons) provide warm-color displays, while lobelia provides the deep blue counterpoint that completes a fully balanced color palette.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Lobelia erinus for Florida Annual Gardens
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Cool-Season Annuals for Gulf South Shade
North Carolina State University Extension — Lobelia erinus Cultivars and Culture

planting.note: Plant corms in fall (September–November in zone 9) for winter and spring bloom; ranunculus corms must be pre-soaked 3–4 hours in cool water before planting to rehydrate the dry corms. Plant with the claw-like corm facing downward, 2 inches deep. The Tecolote series is one of the premier commercial ranunculus series in the world — bred by C. Aris in the Carpinteria, California area, which is one of the world's major ranunculus production centers. In zone 9 ranunculus blooms February–April, producing one of the most spectacular, high-value floral displays of any cool-season bulb. The corms cannot tolerate summer heat — dig and store in a cool, dry location after foliage yellows, or treat as annuals (replace each fall). In zone 9 with good drainage and appropriate timing, ranunculus is one of the most rewarding and spectacular cut flowers that can be grown at home.

directions: Plant in full sun to partial shade in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral, moderately moist soil — ranunculus requires excellent drainage, especially in winter; wet soil causes corm rot; Tecolote Red Ranunculus is a named color selection in the Tecolote Series — one of the finest commercial ranunculus strains available, producing the large, fully double, multi-petaled flowers that represent the highest development of the flower form in Ranunculus asiaticus. The flowers are composed of densely packed, tissue-thin petals in concentric rings that create a structure resembling a perfectly formed, layered rose or peony at 3–4 inches in diameter — the Tecolote Red selection providing vivid scarlet-red flowers with the extraordinary petal density and precision of form that makes Tecolote ranunculus the premium choice for high-end cut flower production and upscale spring garden display. Persian Buttercup (Ranunculus asiaticus) produces some of the most spectacularly beautiful cut flowers available from any garden plant — the large, fully double, multi-layered flowers in vivid, clear colors lasting 1–2 weeks in a vase and providing a level of floral sophistication normally associated with peonies, garden roses, or lisianthus. In zone 9, planted in fall for February–April bloom, ranunculus provides a spectacular spring floral display during the critical late-winter window when few other garden plants produce flowers of comparable quality.
sources: University of California Cooperative Extension — Ranunculus asiaticus Tecolote Series Production
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Ranunculus for Florida Cut Flower Production
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Cool-Season Bulbs for Gulf South Gardens

planting.note: Plant bulbs in fall (October–November in zone 9); in zone 9 pre-chill bulbs in the refrigerator (not freezer) for 6–8 weeks before planting — hyacinths require a cold period for proper flower stalk development that zone 9 winters do not reliably provide. Plant chilled bulbs 4–6 inches deep in December or January after refrigerator treatment. In zone 9 treat hyacinths as annual spring bulbs; the bulbs rarely produce flowering-quality bloom in subsequent years without repeat chilling. The intense, sweet fragrance of hyacinth flowers is among the most powerful and recognized of any spring bulb — a single stem in a small room perfumes the entire space. Blue Jacket produces deep navy-blue to violet-blue flowers in dense, tightly packed spikes — one of the deepest blue colors among commonly grown hyacinth cultivars.

directions: Plant pre-chilled bulbs in partial sun to full sun — morning sun with afternoon shade in zone 9 helps the flowers last longer before heat damage — in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral, moderately moist soil with excellent drainage to prevent bulb rot; Blue Jacket Hyacinth is one of the premier deep blue-purple hyacinth cultivars, producing the intensely fragrant, densely packed flower spikes that define the classic hyacinth form — each spike carrying 30–40 individual, waxy, star-shaped florets in a deep navy-blue to violet-blue color that represents the most saturated blue available in this flower type. The extraordinary fragrance of hyacinth is one of the defining scents of spring gardening — a heavy, sweet, powdery-floral perfume that is immediately recognizable and has inspired perfumers for centuries. Individual flower spikes last 1–2 weeks in the garden and can be brought indoors as cut flowers where the fragrance transforms a room. For zone 9 gardeners, pre-chilled hyacinth bulbs planted in December–January provide a spectacular late-winter flowering display (February–March) in containers, border pots, and garden beds — one of the most reliable ways to bring the intense color and fragrance of hyacinth into the Gulf South spring garden.
sources: Louisiana State University AgCenter — Pre-Chilled Spring Bulbs for Gulf South Gardens
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Hyacinthus orientalis for Florida Gardens
Netherlands Flower Bulb Information Center — Hyacinth Cultivar Guide

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 8–10 weeks before transplanting, or plant transplants in fall; in zone 9 plant transplants September–November for fall and winter bloom, or January–February for spring bloom. Sorbet XP Orange is an All-America Selections winner selected for earlier blooming (15–20 days faster than standard Sorbet series) and the rare, vivid true orange color in viola — distinct from the yellow-orange or peach-orange of most orange viola selections. In zone 9 violas (Viola cornuta) perform far better than pansies (Viola x wittrockiana) in winter and early spring heat — the smaller, more numerous flowers of viola are more heat-tolerant and continue blooming as temperatures rise in March–April, lasting 3–4 weeks longer into spring than pansies. The XP suffix designates early-blooming performance.

directions: Plant in full sun to partial shade — in zone 9 afternoon shade extends the cool-season bloom period before summer heat causes decline — in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral, consistently moist soil with good drainage; Sorbet XP Orange Viola is an All-America Selections award-winning compact viola producing very early, prolific, vivid true-orange flowers in the small, rounded, five-petaled form characteristic of Viola cornuta — a more heat-tolerant, smaller-flowered viola species that outperforms pansies (V. x wittrockiana) in the zone 9 climate where winter temperatures are too mild for pansy to set flower buds reliably and spring warmth arrives before pansies peak. The vivid orange color of Sorbet XP Orange is genuinely rare in violas — most 'orange' viola selections trend toward yellow-orange or salmon rather than the clear, saturated mid-orange that this selection provides — making it a distinctive and visually striking addition to winter and spring cool-season plantings. The extremely high flower count, compact spreading habit, and extended bloom season relative to pansies make Sorbet XP Orange one of the most practically useful and reliable cool-season annual selections for zone 9 winter containers, window boxes, and border plantings, providing color from November through April in the mild, pleasant part of zone 9's garden year.
sources: All-America Selections — Viola Sorbet XP Orange Award
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Cool-Season Annuals for Gulf South Fall and Winter
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Viola cornuta for Florida Winter Annual Gardens
